cit.q: in the neighbourhood when the machine for sugarcane resounds
Tēṉūr of the king rich in chariots, where the machine for sugarcane out-roars the elephant bulls –
while the buckets for the nectar squeezing machines suffered [from emptiness]
entiram cilaikkum tuñcāk kampalai / vicayam aṭūum pukai cūḻ ālaitoṟum / karumpiṉ tīm cāṟu virumpiṉir micaimiṉ
You, who desired, enjoy the sweet juice of the sugarcane in all the halls surrounded by smoke, where the jaggery is being boiled, where the clamour does not cease, and where the machines resound!

sense.note:
A borrowing from Sanskrit yantra (NWS). Throughout the first millennium, authors consistently use it in the sense of machine or mechanism.
Its earliest meaning refers to a device associated with sugarcane pressing and the production of cane sugar, in connection with which Patiṟṟuppattu 19:23 clarifies that this likely refers to a sugarcane press. It is telling that the early sugarcane mills are designated by ālai (from Skt. śālā), while the press is referred to by entiram (from Skt. yantra), which may reflect an Indo-Aryan linguistic connection underlying the technology. According to the old commentary on Patiṟṟuppattu 19: 23 (entiram eṉṟatu ālaiyai) and Maturaikkāñci 258 (karumpiṉ entiram ōtai–karumpiṟku iṭṭa ālaiyiṭattu ōcai), the machine is equated with the place where it is used, most likely referring to the sugarcane press, or, by extension, the mill itself. As for the attestations of a press called yantra, the earliest written record may be the Bower Manuscript (5th–6th century AD) part II, fol. 6r, line 9, where we read: "One should obtain the fresh juice of yellow-fruit nightshade (nidigdhikā) by pressing it in a machine" (nidigdhikāyāḥ svarasaṃ grāhayed yantrapīḍitam). Although Hoernle (1908: 310, s.v. yantra) refers to it as a 'pestle', it is more likely that the term already denotes some kind of juice-pressing device.

cit.q: entrance equipped with machines where not even the moon can enter
gates which possessed arrows in a row of machines

sense.note: Elsewhere it refers to the mechanisms of fortress gates, where authors use it to denote fortification devices; possibly already reflecting Sanskrit influence. Later, more clearly under the influence of Sanskrit literature, mechanised structures of palaces appear in the Maṇimēkalai: a mechanical well (entirakkiṇaṟu, 19: 102) and a mechanised pond (entiravāvi, 28: 7).

listBibl.bibl: Cāminātaiyar, U. Vē., ed. 1935. Puṟanāṉūṟu mūlamum uraiyum. Ceṉṉai: Lā Jarṉal Accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/t5dc0n26b
Cāminātaiyar, U. Vē., ed. 1889. Pattuppāṭṭu mūlamum maturaiyāciriyar pārattuvāci nacciṉārkkiṉiyar uraiyum. 1st edition. Ceṉṉai: Tirāviṭaratnākara Accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/s2jdk2wm5hp
Cāminātaiyar, U. Vē. ed. 1903. Eṭṭuttokaiyuḷ nāṉkāvatākiya patiṟṟuppattu mūlamum paḻaiyavuraiyum. Ceṉṉapaṭṭaṇam: Vaijayanti Accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/t4gn5p19r
